title: The Mega-Mailbag Is Here, and the New Best Picture Front-Runner Is …
format: The morning after the Eagles' Super Bowl rout, Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins size up a dull crop of Super Bowl trailers, then read the PGA and DGA tea leaves: Anora swept the weekend and looks like the Best Picture front-runner again. They run the last decade of winners to prove how little the old rules apply, then producer Bobby Wagner opens a 500-plus-email mega-mailbag on festivals, filmmaker deep dives, against-taste picks, kitchen islands, poker, and how you watch movies with two kids.

segments.heading: The Super Bowl Trailers
segments.note: grading a weak batch of game-day spots

films.title: Mission: Impossible - The Final Reckoning
films.note: The teaser Sean's 3-year-old ID'd as 'Tom Cruise.' Sean counts dozens of beautiful images, the biplane chase chief among them.

films.title: Thunderbolts*
films.note: Amanda watched it twice and thought it looked like garbage, gray computer barf, a reheated Guardians/Suicide Squad team-up. Sean is milder. Long tangent on the title asterisk and the Dark Avengers theory; Wyatt Russell returns as U.S. Agent.

films.title: Jurassic World Rebirth
films.note: An extended version aired; Sean found it fine.

films.title: Lilo & Stitch
films.note: The live-action remake, Sean's pick for best ad of the night, with Stitch running around during the game. His daughter Alice loved it.

films.title: F1
films.note: A 30-second spot, mostly Brad Pitt behind the wheel plus Javier Bardem. Sean notes the tag said 'this summer,' not June, and wonders if it's slipping.

films.title: One Battle After Another
films.note: Invoked as the nightmare scenario: if the PTA spot ever signals a date move, it'll break Sean's heart.

segments.heading: The New Best Picture Front-Runner
segments.note: reading the PGA, DGA, and Critics' Choice results

films.title: Anora
films.note: Won the PGA, DGA (Best Director for Sean Baker), and Critics' Choice over one weekend. Sean puts its odds at 51 percent and notes Baker is positioned to win a record four Oscars for one film (picture, director, writer, editor).

films.title: The Brutalist
films.note: The presumed old-Academy alternative that never materialized at the guilds; Adrien Brody took Critics' Choice Best Actor. Its reading room becomes a running mailbag bit.

films.title: Emilia Pérez
films.note: Read as imperiled after its scandal: minimal critical support and broad opposition. Only one Critics' Choice win; even Zoe Saldaña is called vulnerable, though they still expect her to win.

films.title: Conclave
films.note: Won Critics' Choice Best Ensemble; Sean flatly doesn't believe that vote closed before the Emilia Pérez fallout. A possible SAG/BAFTA overperformer that could reopen the race.

films.title: Nickel Boys
films.note: RaMell Ross won the DGA first-feature award to a standing ovation from all five nominees. Sean pleads for more people to watch it.

films.title: A Complete Unknown
films.note: The nominal traditional pick, but a music biopic has never won Best Picture.

films.title: The Substance
films.note: Demi Moore won Critics' Choice Best Actress; if she takes SAG, a Mikey Madison win gets much harder.

films.title: Wicked
films.note: Jon M. Chu won a second Best Director award of the season at Critics' Choice.

films.title: I'm Still Here
films.note: One of the ballots that matters under the PGA/Oscar preferential count, where Anora sits at No. 2 or 3.

films.title: A Real Pain
films.note: Kieran Culkin won Critics' Choice Supporting Actor.

segments.heading: The Last Ten Best Picture Winners
segments.note: how little the old rules still apply

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: The break point: Sean says nothing in Oscar history predicted it. Cited with Parasite as a case where the actual best movie won.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: Called an ignominious win and not among Guillermo del Toro's best; the del Toro-as-beloved-advocate dynamic is the Baker comparison.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Framed as a whitelash to Oscars So White, winning over a field with no rock-solid Best Picture.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: The 1917-at-the-PGAs year that flipped after SAG. Bong Joon-ho theoretically won four Oscars, though the international award goes to the country, not the filmmaker.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: 'No movie like that ever won before.' A strange year.

films.title: CODA
films.note: First streamer win, the Will Smith slap year. Amanda dismissed it as treacle crap at Sundance, then loved it after her daughter was born.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: A multiversal mom-daughter sci-fi dramedy that won it all, over Amanda's pick, Tár.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: The Spielberg/Schindler's List 'it's his time' win, but formally unusual: an opening montage, a black-and-white final hour set decades later.

films.title: The Living Desert
films.note: The tangent: the only person to win four Oscars in one night is Walt Disney, in 1954, for four different films. This 54-minute nature documentary, essentially a 1950s Planet Earth, was his Best Documentary. Sean watched it at 11 p.m.

segments.heading: The Mega-Mailbag
segments.note: Sean and Amanda, with producer Bobby Wagner reading 500-plus listener emails

films.title: The Silence of the Lambs
films.note: Movie-duo question: Sean floats Hannibal Lecter and Clarice as the pod's dynamic, a little captor-and-prisoner.

films.title: Fight Club
films.note: Amanda's duo answer, on the dual-personas theory. 'We all know I'm Tyler Durden.'

films.title: Godzilla vs. Kong
films.note: A previously floated duo comp for the two hosts.

films.title: Bringing Up Baby
films.note: Where they land: classic screwball, Hepburn and Grant, witty repartee and speed. Sturges energy.

films.title: The Philadelphia Story
films.note: First guess for the Hepburn-Grant comp before they correct to Bringing Up Baby.

films.title: It Happened One Night
films.note: Named in the same screwball-comp breath.

films.title: A Few Good Men
films.note: Bobby's read on the pod: Amanda as Bacon, Sean as Tom Cruise, himself as Weinberg, with Bill as Nicholson.

films.title: Untamed Heart
films.note: Sean's against-taste pick: the Tony Bill romance with Marisa Tomei, Christian Slater, and Rosie Perez that he saw at 11 with his mom. He hasn't revisited it and wants it on 4K.

films.title: Fast Five
films.note: Amanda's known zag; 'everybody knows that.'

films.title: Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2
films.note: Amanda's other surprising-taste pick.

films.title: The Duellists
films.note: The filmography-order question: don't start a Ridley Scott deep dive here, you'll be lost. Begin with the accepted classics, then go chronological.

films.title: Badlands
films.note: For a director with a tight body of work like Malick (eight films), start at the beginning and go straight through.

films.title: Stagecoach
films.note: The John Ford entry point, via the You Must Remember This 'old man is still alive' season: his breakthrough with John Wayne.

films.title: My Darling Clementine
films.note: A pick-and-choose stop on the suggested Ford path after Stagecoach.

films.title: They Were Expendable
films.note: Next on the same abbreviated Ford route.

films.title: The Searchers
films.note: The other anchor of the shortcut Ford filmography.

films.title: Cheyenne Autumn
films.note: Cited as a late Ford film that reverses his own conventions, told with huge empathy for Native people.

films.title: 7 Women
films.note: Ford's entirely female-focused final feature, unusual for him.

films.title: Magnolia
films.note: The PTA question: watch him chronologically, all ten films, no skips. Sean recommends the making-of doc 'That Moment' to watch him work in real time.

films.title: Boogie Nights
films.note: Part of the early PTA run where the autobiography (his mom, his dad) sits right on the surface.

films.title: The Master
films.note: The turn: from here on PTA stops explaining, tells you it's not about Scientology, and makes you figure it out.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: Later mentioned as unimpeded vision, the truck rolling backward down the hill looks exactly as it must have in his head.

films.title: Nosferatu
films.note: On theme-hunting: Sean listened back to his Robert Eggers interview and found Eggers, like PTA, reluctant to talk theme, though that doesn't mean it isn't there.

films.title: Marie Antoinette
films.note: Corey's architecture/inspiration question: Amanda bought purple Chuck Taylor high-tops after the anachronistic pair in the 'I Want Candy' montage. Later a Sofia Coppola example of unimpeded vision.

films.title: Perfect Days
films.note: Named as aspirational interiors she'll never actually live in, complete with an 8-track.

films.title: Deep Red
films.note: One of two vintage first-run posters a Warner Bros. listener sent Sean, a Spanish one-sheet for the Argento film.

films.title: The Candidate
films.note: The other gifted first-run poster, for one of Sean's favorite movies, the Robert Redford film.

films.title: Memento
films.note: Unimpeded-vision question: proof Nolan could realize an odd, technically profound idea early on a small budget.

films.title: Porco Rosso
films.note: A Miyazaki example, and a hit with Sean's 3-year-old son, who wants to fast-forward to any plane. The pig smokes and drinks wine.

films.title: Hereditary
films.note: A quick benefactor-commission aside, the 'that first half is awesome' movie.

films.title: True Romance
films.note: Noted as Christian Slater's other 1993 title, released the same year as Untamed Heart.

films.title: The Gorge
films.note: Miles Teller's new Apple TV+ film, out Friday. Sean can't recommend it; Teller might still take the Eagles' Super Bowl over a box-office hit.

films.title: Maestro
films.note: The Bradley Cooper 'Oscar or a Super Bowl win' anecdote, on the Tom Cruise / what's-next question.

films.title: The Killer
films.note: Sean casts himself as Fassbender's assassin, head down, listening to Illmatic, avoiding eye contact, on the poker-hobby bit.

films.title: The Card Counter
films.note: The other poker-spiral comp: 'unboxing me realizing I'm acting like Oscar Isaac in The Card Counter.'

films.title: I'm Not There
films.note: Sean likens his son on a toy train to Richard Gere's Billy the Kid segment.

films.title: Train Dreams
films.note: The Sundance 'logging one' Amanda keeps mocking and Sean keeps defending; his example of a film he wishes he'd seen in a theater rather than on a screener.

films.title: Paddington in Peru
films.note: One of two kids' movies Sean saw over the weekend and loved; he's taking his son again this week.

films.title: Follow That Bird
films.note: The Sesame Street movie Sean caught at Vidiots with Alice after a birthday party; he calls it sick.

films.title: Interstellar
films.note: The theatrical-future question: its re-release success as evidence moviegoing is eventizing, Record Store Day style.

films.title: Avatar
films.note: Part of the large-format lineage (Avatar to Oppenheimer to the Brutalist) driving the premium-experience business.

films.title: Mickey 17
films.note: On Sean's slate for the day, mentioned in the sign-off.

films.title: M3GAN 2.0
films.note: Amanda, on the marketing, has heard it's the T2 of female-robot movies; she still thinks M3GAN should have performed at the Oscars.

films.title: Captain America: Brave New World
films.note: The Red Hulk movie Sean has to see this week; the guys running a Winter Soldier-style paranoid-thriller comparison against it. Extended riff pretending it adapts the Huxley novel.

films.title: Bridget Jones: Mad About the Boy
films.note: Teed up for next week's episode.

films.title: Blue Moon
films.note: Festival talk: the Linklater/Ethan Hawke film Sean expects at Berlin, one reason he wants to finally hit the European festivals in 2026.

films.title: Nouvelle Vague
films.note: The other Linklater, which Sean expects at Cannes.

TV: The Falcon and the Winter Soldier

Bridget Jones: Mad About the Boy.title: Bridget Jones: Mad About the Boy
